answer:
**In *Traité de Législation Vol. I*, Chapter 13 ("Distinction between an arbitrary regime and a legal regime. Of what constitutes the difference"), Charles Comte explicitly distinguishes between a despotic (or arbitrary) regime and a regime of law (or legal regime).**[^1]

He introduces the distinction as follows: "A distinction has been made between peoples subject to arbitrary powers and those subject to legal powers; and between **despotic governments** and governments that act in conformity with the laws. There is often less difference between the one and the other than one is generally inclined to believe."[^2]

Comte explains that the core difference lies not in the underlying **elements of force** (e.g., ideas, prejudices, needs, passions of rulers, ministers, soldiers, etc.) that compose laws or power—which exist in both regimes—but in whether the **action of this power is described in advance**:

> All these elements of force, whose union forms the law, resemble each other in the two countries: the only difference between the one and the other is that, in one, the action of this power or of this law has been described, in all cases where it is to be exercised; whereas it has not been in the other. In the country where the action of the power has been described in advance, each of the elements of force of which it is composed... regulates its action by the description... In the country where the action of the power has not been described in advance, its movements are more disordered: each of the elements... acts with more or less violence, with more or less partiality.[^3]

In the **arbitrary (despotic) regime**, power acts irregularly without prior description, like a "steam engine that lacked a regulator." In the **legal regime**, prior description acts as a "regulator," making action more uniform—but this regularity does not make bad (oppressive) laws good.[^4]

He summarizes: "To say of a people that it is subject to an **arbitrary regime** is therefore to say nothing other than that it is subject to an irregular and disordered force... To affirm... that a people is subject to a **legal regime**, is to say quite simply that the force or power to which it obeys acts in an equal manner in all similar cases."[^5]

The chapter warns against overvaluing "legal" regimes if the laws themselves are malevolent, using the analogy of a slave-owner regulating whippings as meticulously as a farm code.[^6]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation Vol. I*, Ch. 13, [full chapter](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/13-distinction-entre-un-regime-arbitraire-et-un-regim/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation Vol. I*, Ch. 13, [p-0df0fa](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/13-distinction-entre-un-regime-arbitraire-et-un-regim/translation/#p-0df0fa).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation Vol. I*, Ch. 13, [p-72aa45](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/13-distinction-entre-un-regime-arbitraire-et-un-regim/translation/#p-72aa45).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation Vol. I*, Ch. 13, [p-5bc401](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/13-distinction-entre-un-regime-arbitraire-et-un-regim/translation/#p-5bc401).

[^5]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation Vol. I*, Ch. 13, [p-fadb97](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/13-distinction-entre-un-regime-arbitraire-et-un-regim/translation/#p-fadb97).

[^6]: Charles Comte, *Traité de Législation Vol. I*, Ch. 13, [p-9f5b65](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/13-distinction-entre-un-regime-arbitraire-et-un-regim/translation/#p-9f5b65).
